meta.descriptionTemplate: What each coconut charcoal spec means and what good looks like — ash, fixed carbon, calorific value, and moisture — with a three-tier ash benchmark.

heroSubtitle: Every number on a spec sheet answers a buyer's question: is this good? This page defines each parameter of {{brand}}'s coconut shell shisha charcoal, says which direction is better, and shows what a strong value looks like.

hero.ctaQuoteAria: Request the full specifications by WhatsApp
hero.ctaContactAria: Open the inquiry form to request specifications

intro.p1cont: pillar. It owns what each parameter means and what a good value is; the laboratory procedure behind each figure is covered on the testing methods page, linked at each parameter.
intro.sampleNote: These are specs you can test yourself —

keyFacts.heading: The parameters, defined below
keyFacts.definition: A compact echo of the typical values. Each is defined, given a direction-of-good, and benchmarked below.

tierA.heading: Laboratory-measured parameters (Tier A)
tierA.intro: These five are determined in a laboratory by a defined test method.

tierB.heading: Observed performance indicators (Tier B)
tierB.intro: These describe how the charcoal behaves and looks. They are read under defined conditions, not measured to a laboratory standard.

ash.body: Ash content is the percentage of non-combustible mineral residue left after charcoal fully burns. Lower is better: ash does not produce heat, so a high-ash coal leaves more tray residue, chokes airflow, and can dull a session.
ash.goodValue: {{brand}}'s coconut shell shisha charcoal has a typical ash content of {{ashTypical}}%, with a target of {{ashTarget}}% or lower.
ash.frameworkRefPre: What counts as a "good" ash content is best answered as a graded framework, not a single number — see
ash.frameworkRefPost: , where {{brand}}'s flagship grade sits in the {{factoryBand}} band.

fixedCarbon.body: Fixed carbon is the share of solid combustible carbon that remains after moisture and volatile matter are driven off — the fuel that actually delivers sustained, even heat. Higher is better, and it tracks closely with burn time and heat output.
fixedCarbon.goodValue: {{brand}}'s charcoal has a typical fixed carbon of {{fixedCarbonTypical}}%, with a target of {{fixedCarbonTarget}}% or higher.

moisture.body: Moisture content is the percentage of water held in the charcoal. Lower is better: damp coal is slow to light, can smoke or spit, wastes shipped weight, and is more prone to mold in transit.
moisture.goodValue: {{brand}}'s charcoal has a typical moisture content of {{moistureTypical}}%, with a target of {{moistureTarget}}% or lower.

volatileMatter.body: Volatile matter is the portion that vaporizes and burns off as gas when the charcoal is heated, rather than burning as solid carbon. For shisha, lower is better — high volatile matter is associated with more smoke and off-odors that can carry into the session.
volatileMatter.goodValue: {{brand}}'s charcoal has a typical volatile matter of {{volatileTypical}}%, with a target of {{volatileTarget}}% or lower.

calorific.body: Calorific value is the gross heat released per kilogram when the charcoal is fully combusted, expressed in kcal/kg. Higher means more heat per coal — it underpins how quickly a bowl reaches temperature and how steadily it holds.
calorific.goodValue: {{brand}}'s charcoal has a typical calorific value of {{calorificTypical}} kcal/kg, with a target of {{calorificTarget}} kcal/kg or higher.

burnTime.body: Burn time is how long a single coal sustains usable heat in a session. It is an observed performance indicator, not a standardized laboratory measurement, so it varies with cube size, heat management, and bowl setup.
burnTime.goodValue: {{brand}}'s cubes typically deliver around {{burnTimeTypical}} minutes per session, with a target of at least {{burnTimeTarget}} minutes.

ashColor.body: Ash color is the color of the residue left after a coal burns out — a visual indicator of mineral content and how completely the carbon combusted. A light, uniform ash generally signals low mineral content and a clean burn; dark or sooty ash signals the opposite.

readingCoa.heading: Reading a Certificate of Analysis
readingCoa.body: When the Certificate of Analysis for your batch arrives, read it line by line against the parameters above: ash, moisture, and volatile matter should sit at or below their target bounds; fixed carbon and calorific value at or above theirs. A figure outside a target bound is your cue to ask about the batch before shipment.
readingCoa.definitionLinkIntro: For what a Certificate of Analysis is and who issues it, see
readingCoa.definitionLinkLabel: what a Certificate of Analysis contains
readingCoa.procedureLinkIntro: For the procedures that produce each figure, see
readingCoa.procedureLinkLabel: the testing methods behind the values

items.q: What is a good ash content for shisha charcoal?
items.a: Lower ash is better, because ash does not burn. {{brand}} grades its coconut shell charcoal in three ash tiers: Platinum (1.6–1.9%), Super Premium (1.9–2.3%), and Premium (2.3–2.5+%). Our flagship grade sits in the Platinum band with a typical ash content of {{ashTypical}}%. This three-tier framework — the factory's own rubric, not an industry standard — is the clearest way to judge "good".

Low ash means a higher share of the coal is combustible carbon and less is non-combustible mineral. In practice that means a cleaner burn, less residue in the tray, better airflow through the bowl, and lighter, more uniform ash. {{brand}}'s typical ash content is {{ashTypical}}%.

Calorific value is the gross heat released per kilogram of charcoal when fully combusted, measured in kilocalories per kilogram. Higher means more heat per coal. {{brand}}'s coconut shell charcoal has a typical calorific value of {{calorificTypical}} kcal/kg.

items.q: What burn time should I expect?
items.a: {{brand}}'s cubes typically sustain around {{burnTimeTypical}} minutes of usable heat per session, targeting at least {{burnTimeTarget}} minutes. Burn time is observed under defined conditions rather than measured to a lab standard, so real-world results depend on cube size, heat management, and bowl setup.

items.q: What do the numbers on a COA mean?
items.a: Read each measured value against its target: ash, moisture, and volatile matter should sit at or below their bound; fixed carbon and calorific value at or above. A figure outside its target is your cue to ask about the batch before shipment. The certifications page explains what a Certificate of Analysis is and who issues it.
